learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate critical analysis and synthesis of contemporary early reading theories and apply these skills through the completion of a sequence of quizzes.  (1.2, 2.1, 2.5, 3.3, 3.4, 5.4)

Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of early reading development and assessment through the design of reading lesson and assessment plans that include achievable yet challenging learning goals for students of varying abilities and characteristics. (1.2, 2.1, 2.3, 2.5, 3.1, 3.3, 4.2, 5.1)
Demonstrate clear, logical, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional, and academic conventions.
Demonstrate an understanding of schools as workplaces and be familiar with the statutory, ethical and other requirements that govern the work of teachers in schools.
Demonstrate all teaching and professional requirements of the professional experience placement.

assessments.assessment_title: Policies and procedures for professional experience
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Task 3a 


Students completing ‘Standard’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program:

This course requires you to complete a 10 day (2 week block) placement in a school.


Students completing the ‘Turn to Teaching’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program:

This course requires you to complete a 20 day (4 week block) placement in a school.


Students must read the Policies and Procedures for Professional Experience Placements document and respond to the statement declaration on Blackboard by the due date. Failure to do so will result in a withdrawal of the placement and the awarding of a grade of Fail for the course.

assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of this assessment will result in a failing grade and withdrawal of the professional experience placement.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This task requires you to complete a sequence of three quizzes, drawing on your developing understanding of the essential elements of reading instruction. Quiz 1 focuses on phonological awareness, Quiz 2 focuses on fluency, and Quiz 3 focuses on vocabulary (CC 2.4.1: Practised).

The quizzes are completed in your scheduled tutorials for Week 4 (Quiz 1), Week 6 (Quiz 2), and Week 8 (Quiz 3). The quizzes are paper based and must be answered using a standard pen or pencil. Quiz papers are provided at the start of each quiz, but you must bring your own pen/pencil. You may not use any other resources/devices to complete these quizzes (e.g., supporting materials, computers, phones, smartwatches, etc.).

Marking

Quiz questions are a mix of short and long responses. Marks are earned through demonstrating your depth of understanding for each of the quiz topics. Each quiz in the sequence is marked out of 7, and is weighted at 15%, for a combined total of 45% weighting of the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this assessment. If you do not complete a quiz you will lose 15% of the mark.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Planning early and upper primary reading lessons
assessments.weighting: <dd>55% This assignment is considered the equivalent of a 2,500-word essay </dd>
assessments.task_description:
This assessment task will develop your knowledge of how to teach and assess reading, with a focus on early and upper primary reading. It is split into two parts.

Part 1: Lesson and assessment plans

You are required to plan two standalone reading lessons, with a phonics lesson designed for an early primary context (i.e., Prep, Year 1, or Year 2) and a comprehension lesson designed for an upper primary context (i.e., Year 5 or 6). Each lesson will be supported by your use of an appropriate mentor text.

Select ONE reading-related Content Description per lesson from a set of options that could be taught using your selected mentor texts. Each Content Description will be linked to a given year level in the Australian Curriculum: English. The Content Description you select will determine the year levels for your planning.

Each lesson must include:

  1. Achievable yet challenging learning goals for students, communicated in student-friendly language through learning intention(s) and success criteria.
  2. A description of how you would teach the essential element of reading in the genre of a procedural text that could be picked up and used by other teachers.
  3. Examples of teacher talk, think-alouds, and modelling with one or several mentor texts.
  4. An indication of how student learning would be assessed (please detail how you would meaningfully integrate assessment opportunities into your learning experiences).


Part 2: Theoretical rationale

Write a theoretical rationale to justify the choices made in your lesson and assessment plans. Your theoretical rationale should explain:

  • How you drew on relevant curriculum documents and scholarly literature when planning each part of your lessons.
  • How and why you incorporated differentiated teaching strategies to cater for students with varying abilities and characteristics.
  • Your use of appropriate resources to teach and assess reading (e.g., mentor texts, effective use of Information and Communication Technology).
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must submit the assessment electronically via the assignment link on the course website.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
10 day (2 week block) placement (‘Standard’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program)

20 day (4 week block) placement (‘Turn to Teaching’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program)

You are required to attend a school allocated to you by the university in order to observe teaching practice, school life, students, and develop observation and communication skills within a school context. You are also required to plan and teach segments of lessons and full lessons. 

You will have a 10 day (2 week block) or a 20 day (4 week block) placement towards the end of the semester. 100% attendance is mandatory. You must adhere to the School of Education's policies and procedures for professional experience at all times.

Description of placement (‘Standard’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program cohort):

  • The first 2 to 3 days are to be observation (i.e., “Hands-on” observation helping the teacher and students).
  • During the remaining days, students can start teaching segments of lessons (e.g., 10 mins or 15 mins, half a lesson – this might be the start of the lesson, the conclusion, a lesson quiz, discussion, group activity, explaining a concept, etc).
  • If comfortable with lesson segments, the pre-service teacher can progress to a full lesson.
  • Pre-Service Teachers can be paired together with one Supervising Teacher.


Description of placement (‘Turn to Teaching’ Master of Teaching (Primary) Program cohort):

  • Week 1: Observation of supervising teacher. Planning teaching activities with supervising teacher. 2 - 3 days of "hands-on" observation. Assisting lesson activities. End of week 1, Lesson segments. 
  • Week 2: End of week - full lessons. 
  • Week 3: End of week - Lesson sequence.
  • Week 4: Lesson sequence


Illness preventing attendance during your professional experience must be notified to the placements administrator, education@uq.edu.au, the UQ facilitator, your teacher and the school coordinator. Missed days must be made up and negotiated to the satisfaction of the school. Work is NOT acceptable as a reason for missing an observation day.

Your supervising teacher/s will prepare a report at the end of the two week block (10 days) that will advise on your performance in the school context and will determine your outcome for the professional experience. Students are expected to have read and abide by the School of Education Policies and Procedures Document for Professional Experience Placements.

NOTE: all students are responsible for the collection of original evaluation forms. These are to be uploaded to Blackboard on completion of the practicum. Failure to upload the practicum evaluation forms to the Blackboard site may result in a failing grade being awarded for the course.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of this task without prior negotiation with the Course Coordinator will result in a failing grade for the assessment and course.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1

Overview and history of reading instruction; Contemporary theories of early reading

This lecture and tutorial explore the history of reading instruction, from the ABC method, to phonics, the look-say approach, whole language, balanced literacy, and arriving in the present with a science of reading-informed approach. Preservice teachers also learn about key reading theories underpinning the course, including the simple view of reading, Scarborough's reading rope, and the dual route model.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 Overview and history of reading instruction; Contemporary theories of early reading This lecture and tutorial explore the history of reading instruction, from the ABC method, to phonics, the look-say approach, whole language, balanced literacy, and arriving in the present with a science of reading-informed approach. Preservice teachers also learn about key reading theories underpinning the course, including the simple view of reading, Scarborough's reading rope, and the dual route model.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2

Oral language and reading

This week focuses on oral language. Preservice teachers learn about how oral language skills develop, the components of oral language, the relationship between oral language and reading, and how primary teachers foster these skills in classrooms (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2 Oral language and reading This week focuses on oral language. Preservice teachers learn about how oral language skills develop, the components of oral language, the relationship between oral language and reading, and how primary teachers foster these skills in classrooms (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3

Phonological awareness; Concepts of print; Alphabet knowledge

This lecture and tutorial introduce three important aspects of early reading: phonological awareness, alphabet knowledge, and concepts about print. This lays groundwork for the next lecture and tutorial on phonics (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3 Phonological awareness; Concepts of print; Alphabet knowledge This lecture and tutorial introduce three important aspects of early reading: phonological awareness, alphabet knowledge, and concepts about print. This lays groundwork for the next lecture and tutorial on phonics (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4

Phonics

This week focuses on phonics. Preservice teachers learn how to help children develop their word recognition skills, bringing together their knowledge of decoding and reading high-frequency words. Everyone has undoubtedly heard about phonics before, and this week, preservice teachers learn to teach phonics systematically, explicitly, and synthetically (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4 Phonics This week focuses on phonics. Preservice teachers learn how to help children develop their word recognition skills, bringing together their knowledge of decoding and reading high-frequency words. Everyone has undoubtedly heard about phonics before, and this week, preservice teachers learn to teach phonics systematically, explicitly, and synthetically (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5

Fluency

This week focuses on fluency, which acts as a critical bridge between word recognition processes and comprehension processes. Preservice teachers learn about the aspects of fluency that allow readers to read accurately, quickly, and with great expression, clarity, and phrasing. In the lecture, preservice teachers will be taught about the components of reading fluency, the Australian Curriculum: English content descriptions and achievement standards related to fluency, and are introduced to the multidimensional fluency rubric (Rasinski & Young, 2022) as one of several strategies that can be used to summatively assess reading fluency (CC 2.3.3: Taught). In the tutorial, they will listen to a recording of a child reading and summatively assess their reading fluency using the multidimensional fluency rubric, considering the child’s expression and volume, phrasing, smoothness, and pace (CC 2.3.3: Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5 Fluency This week focuses on fluency, which acts as a critical bridge between word recognition processes and comprehension processes. Preservice teachers learn about the aspects of fluency that allow readers to read accurately, quickly, and with great expression, clarity, and phrasing. In the lecture, preservice teachers will be taught about the components of reading fluency, the Australian Curriculum: English content descriptions and achievement standards related to fluency, and are introduced to the multidimensional fluency rubric (Rasinski & Young, 2022) as one of several strategies that can be used to summatively assess reading fluency (CC 2.3.3: Taught). In the tutorial, they will listen to a recording of a child reading and summatively assess their reading fluency using the multidimensional fluency rubric, considering the child’s expression and volume, phrasing, smoothness, and pace (CC 2.3.3: Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6

Language comprehension and reading comprehension; Comprehending components

Comprehending (or understanding) is the reason people read. This week, preservice teachers will learn about several components of comprehension, including inference, background knowledge, knowledge of grammar structures, knowledge of text structures, and comprehension monitoring, which come together to allow readers to build a strong mental model of the texts they are reading. The lecture explains research showing student understanding, engagement, and academic performance are improved by the explicit comprehension instruction in the context of discipline-specific content (CC 2.4.2: Taught). During the lecture, preservice teachers will work with a partner to select from a range of ‘Core Knowledge’ readers on History and Geography or Science topics, become familiar with the content noting points that were not part of their background knowledge, and consider how they might draw on the Core Knowledge materials to explicitly teach reading comprehension and writing in these disciplines (CC 2.4.2: Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6 Language comprehension and reading comprehension; Comprehending components Comprehending (or understanding) is the reason people read. This week, preservice teachers will learn about several components of comprehension, including inference, background knowledge, knowledge of grammar structures, knowledge of text structures, and comprehension monitoring, which come together to allow readers to build a strong mental model of the texts they are reading. The lecture explains research showing student understanding, engagement, and academic performance are improved by the explicit comprehension instruction in the context of discipline-specific content (CC 2.4.2: Taught). During the lecture, preservice teachers will work with a partner to select from a range of ‘Core Knowledge’ readers on History and Geography or Science topics, become familiar with the content noting points that were not part of their background knowledge, and consider how they might draw on the Core Knowledge materials to explicitly teach reading comprehension and writing in these disciplines (CC 2.4.2: Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7

Vocabulary

This week focuses on vocabulary, with particular emphasis paid to vocabulary depth, breadth, and access. The lecture and tutorial explore three tiers of words and explain why focusing on Tier 2 words is particularly important in the direct teaching of vocabulary. The lecture teaches how to explicitly deliver this important element of reading instruction through discipline specific curriculum areas (CC 2.4.3: Taught), with opportunities for preservice teachers to practice developing and delivering reading instruction resources for this purpose (CC 2.4.3: Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7 Vocabulary This week focuses on vocabulary, with particular emphasis paid to vocabulary depth, breadth, and access. The lecture and tutorial explore three tiers of words and explain why focusing on Tier 2 words is particularly important in the direct teaching of vocabulary. The lecture teaches how to explicitly deliver this important element of reading instruction through discipline specific curriculum areas (CC 2.4.3: Taught), with opportunities for preservice teachers to practice developing and delivering reading instruction resources for this purpose (CC 2.4.3: Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8

Comprehension strategies

This week focuses on several comprehension strategies teachers can teach to enhance the mental models their students build when they are reading. This helps preservice teachers to understand the aspects that make up the language and reading comprehension aspects of the simple view of reading and Scarborough's reading rope. This content once again highlights the complexity of reading instruction in contemporary primary school classrooms (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8 Comprehension strategies This week focuses on several comprehension strategies teachers can teach to enhance the mental models their students build when they are reading. This helps preservice teachers to understand the aspects that make up the language and reading comprehension aspects of the simple view of reading and Scarborough's reading rope. This content once again highlights the complexity of reading instruction in contemporary primary school classrooms (CC 2.4.1: Taught, Practised).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9

Explicit reading instruction; Supporting all readers

This week focuses on how teachers can teach and assess reading using an explicit approach named the gradual release of responsibility. Video clips are shared of several assessment methods mentioned in previous weeks. Preservice teachers will learn about creating learning intentions and success criteria, how to break up learning tasks into manageable chunks (CC 2.2.2: Taught), how these chunks can be logically sequenced (CC 2.2.3: Taught), and how to scaffold student learning (CC 2.2.4: Taught). Following this, the lecture explores different ways to support students who struggle with the elements of reading. In the tutorial, preservice teachers will engage in a comparative judgement task where they compare and discuss key features of explicit phonics instruction (CC 2.2.4 – practised). They will review and revise comprehension lessons using the principles of explicit instruction (CC 2.2.2, 2.2.3: Practised).


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9 Explicit reading instruction; Supporting all readers This week focuses on how teachers can teach and assess reading using an explicit approach named the gradual release of responsibility. Video clips are shared of several assessment methods mentioned in previous weeks. Preservice teachers will learn about creating learning intentions and success criteria, how to break up learning tasks into manageable chunks (CC 2.2.2: Taught), how these chunks can be logically sequenced (CC 2.2.3: Taught), and how to scaffold student learning (CC 2.2.4: Taught). Following this, the lecture explores different ways to support students who struggle with the elements of reading. In the tutorial, preservice teachers will engage in a comparative judgement task where they compare and discuss key features of explicit phonics instruction (CC 2.2.4 – practised). They will review and revise comprehension lessons using the principles of explicit instruction (CC 2.2.2, 2.2.3: Practised).

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 10 To Week 11(05 Oct - 18 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Weeks 10 and 11

'Standard' EDUC7620 preservice teachers are on school professional experience placements in Weeks 10 and 11.

'Turn to Teaching' EDUC7620 preservice teachers are not on school professional experience placements in Weeks 10 and 11.

No lectures or tutorials will be held for EDUC7620 in Weeks 10 or 11.


Learning outcomes: L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Weeks 10 and 11 'Standard' EDUC7620 preservice teachers are on school professional experience placements in Weeks 10 and 11. 'Turn to Teaching' EDUC7620 preservice teachers are not on school professional experience placements in Weeks 10 and 11. No lectures or tutorials will be held for EDUC7620 in Weeks 10 or 11.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12

Links between reading and spelling

This week focuses on how teachers can teach and assess spelling. Preservice teachers learn about the four spelling competencies and various methods of teaching and assessing spelling in classrooms, as well as connections with how teachers teach phonics and decoding (spelling involves encoding).

Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12 Links between reading and spelling This week focuses on how teachers can teach and assess spelling. Preservice teachers learn about the four spelling competencies and various methods of teaching and assessing spelling in classrooms, as well as connections with how teachers teach phonics and decoding (spelling involves encoding).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13

Bringing together the essential elements of reading; Course reflection

This last week teaches preservice teachers how the strategies and topics of reading come together in primary classrooms.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 13 Bringing together the essential elements of reading; Course reflection This last week teaches preservice teachers how the strategies and topics of reading come together in primary classrooms.
